hints: Congruent = identical.
Both shape and size must match.
questions.explanation: **Congruent** = same shape AND same size. Every corresponding side and angle matches exactly.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which is NOT a valid congruence shortcut?}
hints: Three of these are standard congruence rules.
SSA is the 'ambiguous case' — it can produce two different triangles.
questions.explanation: SSS, SAS, ASA, and AAS are all valid. **SSA is NOT** — it can correspond to two different triangles, so it doesn't guarantee congruence.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two triangles are similar with scale factor 4. The smaller has sides 3, 4, 5. The larger has sides:}
hints: Multiply each side by the scale factor.
$3 \times 4, 4 \times 4, 5 \times 4$.
questions.explanation: Scale factor 4 means each side is 4× the corresponding original. $3 \to 12$, $4 \to 16$, $5 \to 20$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two similar triangles have scale factor 3. The ratio of their areas is:}
hints: Areas scale by $k^2$.
questions.explanation: Areas of similar figures scale by $k^2$ where $k$ is the linear scale factor. $k = 3 \Rightarrow k^2 = 9$.

questions.promptTex: \text{To prove two triangles similar, the simplest test requires only:}
choices: Two sides and an included angle
hints: If two angles match, what about the third?
Angle sum forces it.
questions.explanation: **AA** (Angle-Angle) is the simplest: two equal pairs of angles automatically force the third pair to be equal too (since they all sum to 180°). So two pairs is enough for similarity.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which postulate proves two triangles congruent if all 3 corresponding} \\ \text{sides are equal?}
hints: S = side, A = angle. All sides means SSS.
AA proves similarity (not congruence).
questions.explanation: SSS: Side-Side-Side. Three equal sides → triangles are congruent.
steps: SSS = Side-Side-Side: all three pairs of corresponding sides equal.
Other postulates: SAS (two sides + included angle), ASA (two angles + included side), AAS (two angles + non-included side).
AA proves **similarity**, not congruence (similar triangles can be different sizes).

questions.promptTex: \text{Two similar triangles have a scale factor of } 3:5. \\ \text{If the smaller triangle has perimeter } 21, \text{ find the larger's perimeter.}
hints: Perimeter scales with the linear scale factor.
steps: Linear measurements (sides, perimeters) scale by the **scale factor** between similar shapes.
Set up the proportion: $$\dfrac{P_{\text{large}}}{21} = \dfrac{5}{3}$$
Cross-multiply: $3 P_{\text{large}} = 105 \Rightarrow P_{\text{large}} = 35$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two similar triangles have a scale factor of } 2:5. \\ \text{If the smaller has area } 8 \text{ cm}^2, \text{ find the larger's area.}
hints: Area scales with the SQUARE of the linear scale factor.
steps: Areas of similar shapes scale by the **square** of the linear scale factor.
Linear ratio $= 5/2$, so area ratio $= (5/2)^2 = 25/4$.
$$A_{\text{large}} = 8 \cdot \dfrac{25}{4} = 2 \cdot 25 = 50 \text{ cm}^2$$

questions.promptTex: \text{A } 5\text{-ft person casts a } 4\text{-ft shadow. At the same time,} \\ \text{a tree casts a } 36\text{-ft shadow. How tall is the tree?}
hints: Similar triangles: $\dfrac{\text{height}}{\text{shadow}}$ is the same for both.
steps: Sun rays hit the person and the tree at the same angle, creating similar triangles.
Set up the proportion: $$\dfrac{\text{height}}{\text{shadow}} = \dfrac{\text{height}}{\text{shadow}}$$
Cross-multiply: $h = \dfrac{5 \cdot 36}{4} = \dfrac{180}{4} = 45$ ft.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two triangles share an angle. The other two angles of one are } 50° \text{ and } 60°. \\ \text{The other two of the second are } 60° \text{ and } 70°. \text{ Are they similar?}
hints: Use the angle-sum theorem (180°) to find the third angle of each triangle.
Both triangles end up with the same three angles.
questions.explanation: Triangle 1's third angle: $180 - 50 - 60 = 70°$, so its angles are $\{50°, 60°, 70°\}$. Triangle 2's third angle: $180 - 60 - 70 = 50°$, so its angles are also $\{50°, 60°, 70°\}$. Same three angles → similar by AA (in fact AAA).
steps: **Recognize the test.** Two triangles are similar if they share two pairs of equal angles (AA). The angle-sum theorem fills in the third angle automatically.
**Find the third angle of triangle 1.** $180 - 50 - 60 = 70°$. Angles: $\{50°, 60°, 70°\}$.
**Find the third angle of triangle 2.** $180 - 60 - 70 = 50°$. Angles: $\{50°, 60°, 70°\}$.
**Compare.** Same set of angles → similar by AA.

questions.promptTex: \text{Triangle ABC has sides } 4, 6, 8. \\ \text{A similar triangle has its shortest side } 6. \\ \text{What is the longest side of the similar triangle?}
hints: Scale factor = $6/4 = 3/2$. Apply to longest side.
steps: The shortest side of the original is $4$; the shortest of the similar is $6$.
Scale factor: $6 / 4 = 3/2$.
Apply to longest side ($8$): $8 \cdot 3/2 = 12$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which of the following is NOT enough to prove two triangles congruent?}
hints: AAA proves similarity, but the triangles could be different sizes.
Need at least one side to fix the size.
questions.explanation: AAA proves the triangles are similar (same shape) but says nothing about size. SSS, SAS, ASA all fix both shape and size.
steps: **SSS:** all three sides equal → identical triangles ✓ (congruence)
**SAS:** two sides + included angle ✓
**ASA:** two angles + included side ✓
**AAA:** all three angles equal — but a tiny triangle and a giant triangle can have the same angles. Only proves **similarity**, not congruence.
Need at least one side somewhere to fix the size.
